html:
Preventive Maintenance Contracts That Fit a Worcester Winter Calendar

A maintenance contract earns its cost back by catching small problems before a New England winter turns them into big ones. We structure ours around what this climate actually does to a roof over twelve months, not a generic quarterly checklist.

What a Maintenance Contract Actually Covers

Maintenance and inspection are related but not the same line item. An inspection documents condition; a maintenance contract authorizes us to fix the small stuff on the spot — clearing a drain, re-sealing a termination bar, replacing a torn pitch pocket, checking heat cable continuity — without generating a separate work order and change order approval for every minor item. That distinction matters on a facilities budget, because it moves a dozen small fixes a year out of the emergency-repair column and into a predictable annual cost.

Full membrane replacement or major structural repair falls outside contract scope and gets flagged as a separate proposal rather than folded into the maintenance rate — that boundary keeps the contract price predictable for the owner and keeps us from underpricing work that needs its own estimate.

Pricing a Maintenance Contract Per Square Foot Per Year

We price contracts per square foot per year, scaled by roof complexity — a simple single-slope retail roof costs less per square foot to maintain than a roof with heavy rooftop equipment, multiple penetrations, and complex flashing detail. Facilities managers comparing that annual number against what an average winter's worth of emergency calls has cost them in past years usually find the contract lands well under it, especially once after-hours emergency rates are counted into the comparison.

Contracts typically bill quarterly, tied to the visit schedule rather than a single annual invoice, which lines up with how most facilities budgets track operating expense throughout the fiscal year rather than as one lump payment.

The Fall Punch List Before Heat Cables Go On

Late fall, before the first hard freeze, the contract calls for a specific punch list: clear all drains and gutters of leaf and debris buildup, test heat cable continuity at eave lines and known ice-dam trouble spots, check that every rooftop unit curb is sealed, and confirm parapet coping is tight. This is the single most cost-effective visit of the year — a clogged drain or a dead heat cable found in November is a ten-minute fix; the same problem found as a January ice dam is an emergency call plus interior damage.

Photos from this visit become the baseline for comparison against the spring walk, so any freeze-thaw damage that shows up later has a clear before-and-after record rather than a guess at when it happened.

  • Clear drains, scuppers, and gutters of debris
  • Test heat cable continuity at known ice-dam locations
  • Re-seal flashing and termination bar joints showing early wear
  • Check rooftop unit curb seals and insulation
  • Confirm parapet coping and edge metal are secure
  • Log membrane condition photographically for the capital plan file

Extending Membrane Life Through Small, Regular Fixes

Every membrane has a design life, but almost none of them hit it without maintenance. A neglected seam or a small blister left unaddressed for a couple of winters turns into a section that needs early replacement, which pulls capital spend forward years ahead of schedule. Regular small fixes are what keep a membrane tracking toward its full design life instead of falling short of it — which, from a budget standpoint, is the difference between a reroof that's planned five years out and one that shows up as an emergency capital request.

A membrane that's maintained through its full term also holds its warranty standing better, since most manufacturer warranties carry maintenance requirements in the fine print that a neglected roof can end up violating without the owner realizing it.

Contract Tiers for Different Building Types

Hospital and lab buildings around the UMass Chan and Gateway Park area carry more rooftop equipment and tighter operational tolerance, so those contracts run a higher visit frequency — quarterly at minimum, with an added post-storm check. A single-tenant retail building along Route 9 with a simple roof plan can run on a twice-a-year contract without losing coverage. Large-footprint warehouse and distribution roofs near the I-290 and Route 146 interchanges get priced on total square footage first, since the labor time on a flat, low-obstruction roof scales more predictably than on a building full of penetrations.

Mixed-use buildings with retail on the ground floor and offices above split the difference — we typically set those on a three-visit annual schedule, weighted toward fall and spring with a summer check added if the roof carries rooftop solar or heavy mechanical load.

Maintenance Contract Questions We Get

What's the difference between an inspection and a maintenance contract?

An inspection documents condition. A maintenance contract authorizes us to fix small issues on the spot during the visit instead of generating a separate work order for each one.

How is contract pricing calculated?

Per square foot per year, scaled by roof complexity — equipment density, penetration count, and flashing detail all factor in.

What happens during the fall visit specifically?

Drain and gutter clearing, heat cable testing, flashing touch-up, and curb seal checks — all the items that prevent an ice dam before the first hard freeze.

Does maintenance really extend membrane life?

Small regular fixes keep minor wear from compounding into major failures, which is what lets a membrane reach its full design life instead of needing early replacement.

How often should a hospital or lab building be visited versus a retail building?

Hospital and lab buildings typically warrant quarterly visits plus post-storm checks given equipment density; a simple retail roof can run on two visits a year.
